content: I was at the park with my kids when I noticed the sudden, sharp crack of thunder and the unmistakable smell of ozone in the air. Spotting the darkening clouds and flashes in the distance, I quickly pulled my kids away from the open field toward a safer spot under a sturdy tree. Suddenly, a blinding bolt struck just feet away, and despite my efforts to protect them, I was hit directly by the lightning. I collapsed, dazed and burned, but strangely, I quickly noticed that my senses—sight, hearing, smell—were sharper than ever before. For example, I could hear distant birdsong clearly over the usual park noise and even smell the faint scent of rain from miles away. A few days later, I visited a neurologist who confirmed unusual heightened neural activity linked to my senses, something they called “post-lightning sensory augmentation.” While rare and not fully understood, this might explain why some lightning strike survivors report extraordinary changes in perception. Now, every trip to the park feels like a new experience—one where I’m hyper-aware of the world around me, even if I remain skeptical of how much I’ve truly changed. I’m still cautious about sharing this story, knowing it sounds unbelievable, but sometimes real life surprises us in strange ways.
